title: Insights into the Nature of Quantum Emitters in Electron-Irradiated hexagonal Boron Nitride
authors: Mouli Hazra, Anna Rupp, Mohammad N. Mishuk, Josefine Krause, Anand Kumar, Julien Chénedé, Mingi Kang, Bayarjargal N. Tugchin, Marijn Rikers, Thomas Pertsch, Alexander Högele, Tobias Vogl
abstract: Quantum emitters in hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) have emerged as a promising solid-state platform for quantum technology applications. However, a persistent challenge in the field is the unclear origin of many observed emission lines, particularly in the visible range, which can be difficult to distinguish from signals arising from organic or process-induced contamination during sample preparations and handling. This ambiguity limits both the reproducibility of emitter generation and the reliable identification of truly intrinsic quantum defects. This work provides a step-by-step framework to assess whether quantum emitters in electron-irradiated hBN are associated with organic contaminants introduced during sample preparation. We employ hyperspectral imaging, thermal annealing, and oxygen plasma etching to investigate the origin of the green-yellow emitters in electron-irradiated hBN. The combined results not only rule out organic contamination as the source of emission but also provide insight into the spectral variability, thermal stability, and vertical localization of the emitters generated in electron-irradiated hBN that was created without any pre- or post-processing. In addition, our experiments demonstrate the feasibility of creating stable emitters in hBN with thicknesses below 10 nm. These findings provide practical guidance for the identification and controlled implementation of hBN-based single-photon emitters in quantum photonic devices.
summary: This paper investigates the origin of green-yellow single-photon emitters in electron-irradiated hBN. By using thermal annealing and plasma etching, the authors prove these emitters are intrinsic defects rather than surface contaminants. This provides a foundation for using ultra-thin hBN layers in quantum photonic devices.
main_problem: Determining whether green-yellow quantum emitters in electron-irradiated hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) are intrinsic lattice defects or extrinsic organic contaminants.
main_result: The study rules out organic contamination as the source of the emission and demonstrates that these emitters are stable, localized within the top hBN layers, and can be created in flakes thinner than 10 nm.
method: The researchers used hyperspectral imaging, thermal annealing up to 500 C, and oxygen plasma etching for depth-resolved analysis, combined with SEM and AFM.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of exfoliated hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) flakes that have undergone electron-beam irradiation via low-acceleration-voltage SEM.
key_observables: Zero-Phonon Line (ZPL) position, Phonon Sideband (PSB) features, photoluminescence intensity, fluorescence lifetime, and photon purity (g2(0)).
important_parameters: Temperature (4.5 K to room temperature), annealing temperature (up to 500 C), hBN thickness (below 10 nm), and electron beam fluence.
main_assumptions: The analysis excludes spectral data below 550 nm due to hardware-induced attenuation, and assumes circular polarization effectively compensates for arbitrary dipole orientations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows ZPL-filtered intensity maps and spectral histograms at different temperatures; Figure 2 displays PL intensity and lifetime maps after various annealing temperatures; Figure 3 presents AFM and PL maps before and after plasma etching to show depth-dependent emitter stability.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the contamination ambiguity, presents a framework using imaging and annealing to rule out organics, uses plasma etching to determine vertical localization, and concludes with implications for scalable quantum photonics.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it addresses the fundamental reliability and reproducibility of solid-state single-photon sources, which are essential building blocks for quantum photonic networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Hyperspectral images of electron-irradiated hBN. (a–c) Intensity maps of ZPL-filtered emission acquired with an excitation power of 180 µW at temperatures of 4.5 K, 155 K, and RT, respectively. The ZPL and PSB at each pixel were fitted using Lorentzian and Gaussian functions, respectively. The extracted peak positions were compiled into histograms for the ZPL (d–e) at 4.5 K and 155 K, and for the PSB (f–h) at 4.5 K, 155 K, and RT. The ZPL peaks are centered at 548.2 nm (4.5 K) and 548.5 nm (155 K), while the PSB peaks are observed at 570.3 nm (4.5 K), 571.3 nm (155 K), and 573.8 nm (RT), indicating a temperature-dependent spectral shift. (i) Representative spectra from two spatial...

Figure 2: Photophysical characterization of emitters following 30 min annealing in an Ar atmosphere at different temperatures. (a–d) Overlay of PL intensity and lifetime maps after annealing at 50 °C, 200 °C, 300 °C, and 500 °C, respectively. These maps reveal the emergence of thermally activated emitters outside the irradiated region and highlight a fundamental difference in emitter density between irradiated and non-irradiated areas. (e) Representative PL spectra recorded at two different annealing temperatures. (f) Central wavelength and FWHM of the PSB as a function of annealing temperature. (g) Lifetime of a single emitter measured at two temperatures. (h) Average lifetime as a...

Figure 3: Layer-by-layer etching of an hBN flake. PL intensity maps recorded before (a) and after 60 s of oxygen plasma etching (b). (c) Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of the thinnest region of the flake at different stages of plasma etching, showing that 2.6 nm is removed after 60 s of etching. Photophysical properties of emitters labeled A ′ (d–f), B ′ (j–l), and C ′ (g–i), identified in (a). In (d, g, j), overlaid lifetime and PL intensity maps before plasma treatment highlight single emitters (yellow) within the electron-irradiated regions (blue circles).
